sticker.alt: Full sticker design: a long horizontal illustration featuring 22 coral reef creatures and features
sticker._note: referenceWidth/referenceHeight MUST match the actual pixel dimensions of full-sticker.png exactly, or every creature's position will be misaligned. Update these the moment you know the real sticker size.

creatures.name: Forereef coral community
creatures.alt: PLACEHOLDER ALT TEXT for creature 01 — replace with a short visual description
creatures.description: The outer reef is called the fore reef and serves as the primary physical barrier between the outer ocean and lagoon. The forereef on Moorea has experienced many disturbances including bleaching and cyclones. Cyclones can sometimes act as a slate-clearing event, making space for new corals to settle and grow. After the most recent bleaching of 2019, the majority of corals that recovered were from the same genera, Pocillopora, shown here. Another bleaching event more recently resulted in the widespread deaths of these corals, which are now sitting on the reef, hollowing out from within.
papers: Burgess, S. C. et al. (2021). Response diversity in corals: Hidden differences in bleaching mortality among cryptic Pocillopora species. Ecology, 102(6), e03324.
Scafidi, K. C. et al. (2026). Remnant hollowed out dead coral skeleton branches defer coral community recovery. Plos one, 21(3), e0339527.

creatures.name: Fringing reef coral community
creatures.alt: A cluster of light brown corals with sea urchins around them
creatures.description: The reef closest to the island is called the fringing reef. This ecosystem is first to absorb any pollutants that run off the land into the water and, in Moorea, is characterized by many corals from the genus Porites. Here you ca see the Porites rus corals creating a habitat for sea urchins, which can be found in high abundance in these nearshore environments.
papers: Edmunds, P. J. et al. (2025). Long‐term community dynamics are heterogeneous between fringing‐and fore‐reef habitats on an Indo‐Pacific coral reef. Ecosphere, 16(10), e70398.

creatures.name: Poti Marara
creatures.alt: A red fishing boat, moving quickly over the water.
creatures.description: "Poti" means boat in Tahitian, and "marara" means flying fish, illustrating the speed of these vessels as their V shaped hull cuts seamlessly into the water. With a bow-positioned tiller, the driver is able to steer the boat with one hand, and spear fish from their high vantage point with the other. The front tiller steering allows for tight turns and easy mobility.
papers: Watch video

Blanchet, G. et al. (1988). The "poti marara" or the success of a small fishing boat fitted to needs. In Proceedings of the World Symposium on Fishing Gear and Fishing Vessel Design (pp. 244–246). Marine Institute. PDF Here

creatures.name: Magnificent sea anemone
creatures.alt: PLACEHOLDER ALT TEXT — replace with a short visual description
creatures.description: The magnificent sea anemone is a charismatic creature on the reef, easily recognizable thanks to the mutually beneficial relationship between these anemone and the fishes that live on and around them. Since sea anemones are in the Phylum Cnidaria (the same one as sea jellies), they have similar stinging cells, called cnidocytes, located in their tentacles. So be careful not to touch these mesmerizing creatures, in case your skin reacts to the sting.
papers: PLACEHOLDER — replace with a citation for a relevant scientific paper, or leave blank.

creatures.name: Polynesian anemonefish
creatures.alt: PLACEHOLDER ALT TEXT for creature 07 — replace with a short visual description
creatures.description: Anemonefish have an iconic muralist relationship with their hosts> The fishes gain physical protection for themselves and their clutches of eggs, while the fishes protect their hosts from other fish predators.
papers: O’Donnell, J. L. et al. (2025). A new species of Anemonefish from French Polynesia, Amphiprionmaohiensis,(Pomacentridae, Amphiprioninae), the Polynesian anemonefish. ZooKeys, 1244, 225.
Schligler, J. et al. (2022). Aggression of an orange-fin anemonefish to a blacktip reef shark: a potential example of fish mobbing? Marine Biodiversity 52:17.

creatures.name: Coral Bommie with Macroalgae
creatures.alt: PLACEHOLDER ALT TEXT — replace with a short visual description
creatures.description: In the shallow lagoons of Moorea, corals form large structures called "bommies", which are constructed of living and dead coral skeletons and can be multiple meters in diameter. These structures form ideal habitat for a diverse community of marine organisms, especially other invertebrates. Bommies can also be colonized by macroalgae, such as Turbinaria ornata as is shown here. This particular species of macroalgae is extremely hardy and is not eaten by many fishes, especially once it grows to full size, making it a strong competitor with corals for empty space on the reef.
papers: Schmitt, R. J. et al.(2022). Evaluating the precariousness of coral recovery when coral and macroalgae are alternative basins of attraction. Limnology and Oceanography, 67, S285-S297.
Bittick, S. J., Bilotti, N. D., Peterson, H. A., & Stewart, H. L. (2010). Turbinaria ornata as an herbivory refuge for associate algae. Marine Biology, 157(2), 317-323.

Cheh, A. S. et al. (2024). Algal turf accrues both costs and benefits to growth via association with adjacent stands of Turbinaria ornata, a rapidly expanding macroalga in the South Pacific. Marine Biology, 171(10), 200

creatures.name: Humpback whale
creatures.description: Whale season is from roughly the middle of June until November in Moorea. During this time, you can hear the whales communicating underwater while diving, and may be lucky enough to see them breaching (jumping above the water) offshore throughout the day. Occasionally, the mothers will bring their babies near the reef, protecting them from predators by using the structural boundary of the reef as a natural barrier on one side.
papers: Raick, X. et al. (2021). From the reef to the ocean: revealing the acoustic range of the biophony of a coral reef (Moorea Island, French Polynesia). Journal of Marine Science and Engineering, 9(4), 420.
